coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of stable platinum nanoclusters with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for sensitive detection of glucose and glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Liyuan
Ji, Xianbing
Lin, Guoqing
Liu, Kai
Chen, Shengfu
Ma, Guanglong
Xue, Weili
Zhang, Xiaoyu
Wang, Longgang
coredata.dc:description: The sensitive and facile detection of the concentration of glucose and glutathione (GSH) is important for our health. In this work, lily polysaccharide (LP) stabilized platinum nanoclusters (Ptn-LP NCs) were prepared by a facile synthetic strategy where LP was used as a reducing agent and stabilizer. The successful preparation of 1–2 nm platinum nanoclusters (Pt NCs) inside of Ptn-LP NCs was confirmed using UV–Vis spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), respectively. Furthermore, Ptn-LP NCs accelerated the catalytic oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), indicating their intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. The V m values of TMB and H2O2 were 50.3 × 10−8 Ms−1 and 10.2 × 10−8 Ms−1, respectively, which were higher than those of other mimetic enzymes. The peroxidase-like property of Ptn-LP NCs was used to sensitively detect the concentration of glucose and glutathione (GSH). The detection limits of glucose and GSH were as low as 1.89 and 0.37 μM, the linear ranges of glucose and GSH were 2.5–1000 and 4–140 μM, respectively. The colorimetric method was successfully applied to detect GSH concentration in human serum. This method has great potential application in bio-related detection in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Peroxidase-like activity
Colorimetric detection
Platinum
Glucose
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-like activity assay of Pt10-LP NCs
Preparation and characterization of Ptn-LP NCs
Detection of glucose using Pt10-LP NCs
Detection of GSH using Pt10-LP NCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectra and (b) corresponding photographs of (1) LP, (2) K2PtCl4, (3) Pt10-LP NCs, (4) Pt20-LP NCs and (5) Pt40-LP NCs, respectively, the XPS spectrum of (c) Pt10-LP NCs and (d) the binding energy of Pt 4f.
LP and Ptn-LP NCs (n = 10, 20, 40) at pH 7: (a) hydrodynamic sizes and (c) zeta potentials, Pt10-LP NCs in different buffer (pH = 1–10): (b) hydrodynamic sizes and (d) zeta potentials.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs (insert corresponding substrates ABTS, TMB and OPD solution), (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs at 652 nm: (1) Pt10-LP NCs + TMB + H2O2, (2) TMB + H2O2, (3) Pt10-LP NCs + TMB (insert corresponding solution) and (c) corresponding time-varying curves.

Steady-state kinetic curves of Pt10-LP NCs: (a) 11.11 mM H2O2 with different concentrations of TMB; (c) 0.4 mM TMB with different concentrations of H2O2; (b) and (d) were double reciprocal plots of (a) and (c), respectively.

Pt10-LP NCs catalyzed oxidization of TMB at various GSH concentration (0.2–160 μM): (a) UV–Vis spectra at 652 nm, (b) the response curve and linear calibration plots for GSH detection (insert corresponding solution) and (c) selectivity of colorimetric detection of GSH using Pt10-LP NCs.
Comparison of kinetic parameters of K m and V m of HRP and mimetic enzymes.
Comparison of different biosensor for the detection of glucose.
Comparison of different biosensor for the detection of GSH.
GSH recoveries detected in human serum sample.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of stable platinum nanoclusters with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for sensitive detection of glucose and glutathione

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biomass Chemical Engineering of Ministry of Education
College of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biomass Chemical Engineering of Ministry of Education, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection limits of glucose and glutathione using lily polysaccharide stabilized platinum nanoclusters were as low as 1.89 and 0.37 μM, respectively.

The sensitive and facile detection of the concentration of glucose and glutathione (GSH) is important for our health. In this work, lily polysaccharide (LP) stabilized platinum nanoclusters (Ptn-LP NCs) were prepared by a facile synthetic strategy where LP was used as a reducing agent and stabilizer. The successful preparation of 1–2 nm platinum nanoclusters (Pt NCs) inside of Ptn-LP NCs was confirmed using UV–Vis spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), respectively. Furthermore, Ptn-LP NCs accelerated the catalytic oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), indicating their intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. The V m values of TMB and H2O2 were 50.3 × 10−8 Ms−1 and 10.2 × 10−8 Ms−1, respectively, which were higher than those of other mimetic enzymes. The peroxidase-like property of Ptn-LP NCs was used to sensitively detect the concentration of glucose and glutathione (GSH). The detection limits of glucose and GSH were as low as 1.89 and 0.37 μM, the linear ranges of glucose and GSH were 2.5–1000 and 4–140 μM, respectively. The colorimetric method was successfully applied to detect GSH concentration in human serum. This method has great potential application in bio-related detection in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: The glucose is energy material for our body. The abnormal concentration of glucose is bad for our health. Meanwhile, glutathione (GSH) is an essential antioxidant in organism with strong reducibility. GSH is one type of tripeptide thiols and is composed of glutamic acid, cysteine and glycine [1]. The GSH concentration is closely related to human health, and the unbalance of GSH causes a variety of diseases such as cancer, heart disease, hepatic damage [2]. Thus, the sensitive and facile detection of the concentration of glucose and GSH is important. Colorimetric method has attracted wide attention to determine the concentration of glucose and GSH [3,4].

Natural enzymes, especially peroxidases, are extensively applied in colorimetric method. Natural enzymes have highly efficient catalytic ability and excellent selectivity [5–8]. Nevertheless, natural enzymes are associated with many defects, such as high-cost extraction, easy deterioration, and poor stability under harsh conditions, which restrict their applications. To overcome these shortages, various kinds of artificially prepared materials with enzyme-like activities have been developed. These mimetic enzymes include metallic oxides [9], grapheme [10], noble metal-based nanomaterials [11] and metal-organic frameworks [12,13]. Noble metal-based nanomaterials have good catalytic performance and stability without oxidation [14]. It has been reported that Pt nanomaterials possessed excellent catalytic performance among them [15]. For example, Fe3O4-Pt/core-shell nanoparticles (MPt/CS NPs) possess superior peroxidase-activity, which is attributed to their good binding ability to substrate TMB and very fast reaction rate [16]. Hierarchically structured platinum nanoparticles with a small diameter (30 nm) are prepared by a seed-mediated growth method, which show an excellent catalytic performance owing to the large specific surface area and more active sites [17]. Small metal nanoparticles have a large proportion of exposed atoms as active sites, and exhibit high catalytic activity. However, the large particle size and low stability in solution restrict the application of many metal nanomaterials [18].

Recently, ultra-small and stable metal nanoparticles in solution have attracted extensive attention [19]. Green synthesis using natural extracts such as Cucurbita pepo leaf extract has been successfully used to prepare metal nanoparticles [20]. However, the components of natural extracts are complex and have different reducing ability. One single component of natural extracts is more suitable for controlled synthesis of metal nanoparticles. Polysaccharide is a soluble component of natural extracts [21,22]. Zhao and coworkers isolated lily polysaccharide (LP) from Lilium davidii var. unicolor Salisb by microwave-assisted extraction method. LP possesses large molecule weight and many biological active sites [23], such as a large number of aldehyde and hydroxyl groups, which play a significant role in antioxidant and anti-aging ability. LP provides several advantages, such as water-solubility, good antioxidant and a wide source of raw materials, which are beneficial to used as a green reducing agent and stabilizer [24].

In this work, LP reacted with K2PtCl4 to prepare lily polysaccharide stabilized platinum nanoclusters (Ptn-LP NCs) without additional ligands for the first time. LP was used as a reducing agent and stabilizer in this reaction. This green method is friendly to environment and economics [11]. Ptn-LP NCs was proved to possess superior peroxidase activity which was confirmed by catalyzing H2O2 to convert colorless TMB to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB). Based on peroxidase-like activity of Ptn-LP NCs, the colorimetric method for the sensitive determination the concentration of glucose and GSH was constructed.

Dried lily powders were extracted twice by hot water (wlily powders:wwater = 1:20) at 80 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the extracted solution was centrifuged and added 30% H2O2 (Vextracted solution:V30% H2O2 = 1:10) at 50 °C for 2 h. The Sevage reagent was further mixed with obtained solution. The mixed solution was stirred about 10 min and centrifuged. After repeated steps to remove proteins, the aqueous solution was collected and dialyzed against deionized water for 6 times to remove impurities. The obtained LP solution was freeze-dried to get final product.

The LP solution (1 mg/mL) was mixed with K2PtCl4 solution (2 mM) and further incubated in the Thermo Cell at 70 °C for 12 h. The different molar ratio of [K2PtCl4]/[LP] was 10, 20 and 40, respectively. The mixed solution dialyzed against deionized water to remove impurities to get Ptn-LP NCs (n = 10, 20 and 40) solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity assay of Pt10-LP NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Typical peroxidase substrate TMB, ABTS and OPD were used to study the catalytic activity of Pt10-LP NCs. There were three groups as follows: (i) Pt10-LP NCs + TMB + H2O2, (ii) TMB + H2O2, and (iii) Pt10-LP NCs + TMB. As for Pt10-LP NCs + TMB + H2O2, 900 μL of TMB (0.6 mM), 50 μL of H2O2 (300 mM), 350 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 4) and 50 μL of Pt10-LP NCs (cPt = 0.264 mM) were mixed and incubated at 25 °C for 5 min. Then the absorbance of reaction system was measured.

To verify the effect of pH on peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs, the experimental procedures were applied as follows. 50 μL of Pt10-LP NCs (cPt = 0.264 mM) and HAc-NaAc buffer were incubated at 40 °C for 5 min in the Thermo Cell. After that, 900 μL of TMB which was dissolved in different pH (1–12) HAc-NaAc buffer was added and incubated at 40 °C for 5 min. Subsequently, 50 μL of H2O2 (300 mM) was added into above solution and incubated at 40 °C for 3 min. Finally, the absorbance of mixed solution was measured at 652 nm by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Meanwhile, the effect of temperature on the peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs had similar experimental procedures. The temperature ranged from 20 to 80 °C.

The effects of TMB concentration (0.089–0.44 mM) or H2O2 concentration (0.22–3.11 mM) on the catalytic activity of Pt10-LP NCs were studied, respectively. When the concentration of H2O2 was fixed, 100 μL of H2O2 (300 mM), 100 μL of Pt10-LP NCs (cPt = 0.264 mM) and HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4) were incubated at 40 °C for 5 min. And 200–1000 μL of TMB (0.6 mM) were added to obtained solution, respectively. Meanwhile, when the concentration of TMB was fixed, 900 μL of TMB (0.6 mM), 50 μL of Pt10-LP NCs (cPt = 0.264 mM) and HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4) were mixed at 40 °C for 5 min. And then 10–140 μL of H2O2 (30 mM) were added to obtained solution, respectively. The absorbance at 652 nm of final solution was recorded with different time. The kinetic parameters K m and V m were obtained by using Michaelis-Menten equation.

The peroxidase-like mechanism of Pt10-LP NCs was further investigated as follows. 900 μL of terephthalic acid (TA) (0.5 mM), 100 μL of H2O2 (300 mM), 300 μL of Pt10-LP NCs (cPt = 0.264 mM) and 200 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4), which were incubated at 30 °C for 12 h in the Thermo Cell. The control groups were also measured.

To investigate the standard curve of glucose detection, GOx and glucose were dissolved in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.2), respectively. 100 μL of GOx (5 mg/mL) and 200 μL of glucose (2.5–1000 μM) were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 200 μL of Pt10-LP NCs solution (cPt = 0.264 mM) and 900 μL of TMB (0.6 mM) were incubated at 40 °C for 20 min. Next, these samples were mixed together and reacted at 40 °C for 30 min. Finally, the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded. To study the interference tests for other sugars (10 mM), fructose, maltose and lactose were measured using the method of glucose detection.

Pt10-LP NCs were also used to construct a standard curve for the detection of GSH by a colorimetric method. First, 100 μL of Pt10-LP NCs (cPt = 0.264 mM) and 900 μL of TMB (0.6 mM) were mixed with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4) and incubated at 40 °C for 5 min. Additional 100 μL of H2O2 (30 mM) was added. After 5 min, 415 μL of GSH (0.2–160 μM) were added and reacted 40 °C for 3 min. The absorbance of final solution was measured at 652 nm. Furthermore, this method was used to measure other 1400 μM interfering substance (amino acids and metal ions) as the control groups.

The Pt10-LP NCs were applied to detect the content of GSH in human serum samples. The fresh human blood samples were obtained from a healthy volunteer and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 5 min. The human serum was collected and diluted to half of the original concentration. Then, the samples were mixed with different concentration of GSH (10, 20 and 30 μM) by the standard addition approach, respectively. The samples were measured by using standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Ptn-LP NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Polysaccharides are biological macromolecules. LP is regarded as biological template which provides active sites such as hydroxyl groups and aldehyde groups. These active sites may efficiently reduce metal ions and stabilize metal nanoparticles. Ptn-LP NCs were synthesized in aqueous solution by a green synthetic method. Fig. 1 (a) showed UV–Vis spectra of LP, K2PtCl4 and Ptn-LP NCs. K2PtCl4 had two broad characteristic peaks at 329 and 392 nm in the wavelength from 400 to 800 nm. Both peaks disappeared after the incubation of K2PtCl4 and LP at 70 °C for 12 h. When the molar ratio of K2PtCl4 to LP increased, the absorbance of Ptn-LP NCs had a growing tendency. In addition, Fig. 1(b) exhibited the color change of solution: colorless LP and brown Ptn-LP NCs. The brown solution of Ptn-LP NCs was clear without precipitation. These results demonstrated that Ptn-LP NCs (n = 10, 20 and 40) were prepared.

The size of metal nanoparticle had an obvious effect on their catalytic activity. To further investigate the formation of Pt NCs, the size of as-prepared Pt NCs inside of Ptn-LP NCs was measured by TEM and size distribution curves were fitted by Gauss function. As shown in Fig. S2, Ptn-LP NCs exhibited excellent monodisperse states without agglomeration. Meanwhile, the average sizes of Pt NCs inside of Ptn-LP NCs were 1.27 ± 0.35 nm for Pt10-LP NCs, 1.34 ± 0.37 nm for Pt20-LP NCs and 1.42 ± 0.33 nm for Pt40-LP NCs, respectively. It was found that the diameters of as-prepared Pt NCs were within 1–2 nm which were smaller than those of most the other previously reported Pt nanoparticles [25]. Finally, Pt10-LP NCs were selected as the catalyst for subsequent characterization, which was mainly because of the largest specific surface area in the Ptn-LP NCs [19,26].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was used to reveal the valence states of elements of Pt10-LP NPs. As shown in Fig. 1(c), there were three sharp peaks corresponding to C 1s at 284.6 eV, O 1s at 532.8 eV and Pt 4f. And the lack of peak for N 1s demonstrated that extracted LP had high purity without containing proteins which contained a lot of nitrogen elements. As shown in Fig. 1(d), two broad peaks at 74.21 and 70.80 eV were typical for Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 of metallic Pt(0), respectively. The binding energy of Ptn-LP NCs was also in accordance with previously obtained values for zero valent Pt nanoparticles [27]. To sum up, these data revealed that the Pt(Ⅱ) of K2PtCl4 was reduced to Pt(0) and the Pt NCs were stabilized by LP.

The aggregation of nanomaterials in solution directly reduces their catalytic activity and application [28]. Small nanoparticles without stabilization easily aggregated, which mainly resulted from their high specific surface energy. Hence, it was essential to detect the hydrodynamic sizes of prepared Ptn-LP NCs using DLS [29]. Fig. 2 (a) showed that the hydrodynamic sizes of LP, Pt10-LP NCs, Pt20-LP NCs and Pt40-LP NCs were about 43.0 ± 8.7, 41.5 ± 7.1, 52.1 ± 4.4 and 47.6 ± 13.0 nm in buffer (pH = 7), respectively. The hydrodynamic sizes of Ptn-LP NCs (n = 10, 20 and 40) remained almost unchanged on the 7th day. Furthermore, Pt10-LP NCs were incubated in different buffer (pH = 1–10) to study their stability by DLS [30]. As shown in Fig. 2(b), it was observed that the hydrodynamic sizes of Pt10-LP NCs were between 36.5 and 43.2 nm without significant changes on the 7th day. Thus, Pt10-LP NCs maintained high stability in a wide pH range for a long time. In comparison with the measured diameters of Ptn-LP NCs by TEM, the tested hydrodynamic sizes by DLS was much bigger. The reason is that the method of TEM reflected the size of Pt in dried state, while the DLS method reflected the size of Ptn-LP NCs including LP in solvated states. Moreover, Fig. 2(c) displayed that the zeta potentials of LP, Pt10-LP NCs, Pt20-LP NCs and Pt40-LP NCs were −4.2 ± 0.6, −4.4 ± 2.3, −1.9 ± 1.1 and −1.5 ± 1.1 mV at pH = 7, respectively. Pt10-LP NCs had the zeta potentials in the range from −0.5 to −4.4 mV in different buffer (pH = 1–10) as shown in Fig. 2(d). Thus, Pt10-LP NCs had small zeta potential values and high stability. The high stability of Pt10-LP NCs should be due to the presence of LP which provided enough steric hindrance. All of results indicated that Ptn-LP NCs preformed excellent dispersibility and good stability in harsh conditions. Therefore, good stability of Ptn-LP NCs was beneficial to highly efficient catalytic activity in solution.

TMB, ABTS and OPD were typical chromogenic substrates which were employed to further investigate the peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), when each solution was added Pt10-LP NCs and H2O2, the solution of TMB, ABTS and OPD occurred characteristic absorbance at about 652, 420 and 450 nm, the color also changed from colerless to blue, bluish green and orange, respectively. Furthermore, the catalytic ability of Pt10-LP NCs was carefully studied by using TMB as substrate. As shown in Fig. 3(b), it was apparently seen that system [Pt10-LP NCs + TMB] did not have obvious absorption peak and color change of mixed solution, indicating that TMB was not oxidized (curve 3). Furthermore, the system [TMB + H2O2] was observed a minor absorbance peak at 652 nm corresponding to very light blue solution (curve 2). However, system [TMB + Pt10-LP NCs + H2O2] exhibited a maximum absorbance peak and blue oxTMB (curve 1). As shown in Fig. 3(c), the absorbance of system [TMB + Pt10-LP NCs + H2O2] reached peak value in less than 5 min (curve 1), indicating that the reaction finished within 5 min. All results proved that Pt10-LP NCs had peroxidase-like activity.

Next, the effect of solution pH (1–10) and reaction temperature (20–80 °C) on the peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs was studied. As shown in Fig. S3(a), the catalytic activity of Pt10-LP NCs exhibited the maximal catalytic activity at pH = 4. Compared with neutral or alkaline solution, the weak acid pH was good for catalytic reaction of Pt10-LP NCs. In addition, Fig. S3(b) suggested that Pt10-LP NCs exhibited the biggest catalytic activity at 40 °C. On the basis of above results, the catalytic activity of Pt10-LP NCs was dependent on pH and temperature; the optimized experimental conditions were pH = 4 and 40 °C.

To further investigate the peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs, the effect of concentration of TMB or H2O2 on the steady-state kinetics was studied. Fig. 4 (a) and (c) confirmed typical Michaelis-Menten curves of TMB and H2O2, respectively. Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plots displayed in Fig. 4(b), (d) were used to calculate Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximal reaction velocity (V m) in Eq. (1). K m reflected the binding affinity of enzyme and substrates.

The K m and V m of Pt10-LP NCs, HRP and other previously reported mimetic enzymes were summarized in Table 1 . The calculated K m values of Pt10-LP NCs for TMB and H2O2 were 0.265 and 2.64 mM, respectively, which were lower than those of HRP and Au NCs in the presence of Pb2+. Thus, Pt10-LP NCs had higher affinity towards TMB and H2O2 as substrates. Besides, the V m values with TMB (50.3 × 10−8 Ms−1) and H2O2 (10.2 × 10−8 Ms−1) were much higher than those of HRP (10.0 × 10−8 Ms−1, 8.71 × 10−8 Ms−1) and CoSe2/rGo (3.4 × 10−8 Ms−1, 1.25 × 10−8 Ms−1). Thus, Pt10-LP NCs had enhanced peroxidase-like activity, which should be due to ultra-small size of Pt NCs inside of Pt10-LP NCs and high stability in solution.
(1)
v =
V m
S
/
K m
+
S
where v is the initial velocity, V m is maximal reaction velocity, K m is Michaelis-Menten constant and [S] represents substrates concentration.

Catalytic oxidation of TMB may be related with the generation of hydroxyl radical (OH) by peroxidase-assisted H2O2 decomposition. TA as a fluorescent probe was employed to replace TMB and study the role of prepared Pt10-LP NCs when they were treated with H2O2. Non-fluorescent TA combined with OH to generate highly fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid which had an obvious peak at 440 nm [37].

As shown in Fig. S4(a), line 3 [TA + Pt10-LP NCs] and line 2 [TA + H2O2] showed low fluorescent intensity at 440 nm, but line 1 [TA + H2O2 + Pt10-LP NCs] had the highest fluorescent intensity at 440 nm. As shown in Fig. S4(b), the absorbance value of line 1, line 2 and line 3 at 440 nm were 4528, 976 and 110.5, respectively. The absorbance value of line 1 was much higher than that of the other lines. Both results indicated more OH was generated in the line 1 [TA + H2O2 + Pt10-LP NCs]·H2O2 and Pt10-LP NCs were two important factors for the formation of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid. The fact further proved that Pt10-LP NCs promoted the decomposition of H2O2 to form more OH, and TMB combined with OH to produce oxTMB [38,39]. Thus, Fig. 3(c) showed the absorbance value of line 1 [Pt10-LP NCs + TMB + H2O2] at 652 nm was higher those of the other lines. H2O2 and TMB should be absorbed on surface of Pt NCs of Pt10-LP NCs, and surface reaction should be the determining step [40]. Apparently, the generation of OH played an indispensable role in the peroxidase-like function of Pt10-LP NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose using Pt10-LP NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of good peroxidase-like activity of Pt10-LP NCs, a sensitive biosensor by a colorimetric method for rapidly detecting glucose was developed. Glucose reacted with glucose oxidase (GOx) and oxygen (O2) in the air to produce H2O2, and then the colorimetric reaction of Pt10-LP NCs, TMB and obtained H2O2 occurred. As shown in Fig. S5(a), the absorbance value of oxTMB at 652 nm increased with increasing glucose concentration from 2.5 to 1000 μM, and the blue color of mixed solution gradually deepened. The glucose concentration response equation was A = 0.0009124cglucose + 0.06665, this equation had good linear relationship (R2 = 0.998). The linear range was from 2.5 to 1000 μM and the detection limit (LOD) of glucose was 1.89 μM (S/N = 3). The linear range was wide and the LOD was lower than those of other previous catalysts [41–45] in Table 2 .

Furthermore, other sugars such as lactose, maltose and fructose were selected to investigate the selectivity of the biosensor system by the colorimetric approach. The concentration of other sugars (10 mM) was 10 times than that of glucose (1 mM). As shown in Fig. S5(b), the absorbance value of other sugars was much lower than that of glucose, which was owing to the high binding affinity of GOx towards the glucose. Thus, the results proved the good selectivity of colorimetric method based on Pt10-LP NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH using Pt10-LP NCs
ce:section.ce:para: GSH is extensively existed in animals or plants and enable to maintain normal immune system function in organisms. An abnormal concentration of GSH could lead to various diseases. As a result, the detection of GSH concentration is of great importance for our health. Herein, the colorimetric method for the detection of GSH was developed using Pt10-LP NCs. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), as GSH ranging concentrations from 0.2 to 160 μM was added into the system [Pt10-LP NCs + TMB + H2O2], the absorbance value of oxTMB at 652 nm gradually decreased, and the color of solution faded which can be seen by naked eyes. When the concentration of GSH was beyond 140 μM, the blue solution was eventually converted to be colorless. The main reason was that oxTMB was reduced to TMB by additional GSH [46]. Thus, a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9998) exhibited between GSH concentration ranging from 4 to 140 μM in Fig. 5(b). The obtained formula for the detection of GSH was ΔA = 0.00457cGSH + 0.17665 (ΔA = Ablank − Aadded GSH), and the LOD was calculated to be 0.37 μM (S/N = 3). These results strongly proved that the sensor based on Pt10-LP NCs possessed excellent sensitivity compared with Fe3O4 NPs [47], H2TCPP-Co(OH)2-GO [48] and other reported GSH-biosensors [49–52] as listed in Table 3 .

To further examine the selectivity of the colorimetric biosensor for GSH detection, various kinds of amino acid molecules (Phe, Tyr, Ala, Lys, Pro, Val, His, Leu) and metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, NO3 −) were used as interfering agents. The concentration of interfering agents was 1400 μM which was 10 times higher than that of GSH (140 μM). As shown in Fig. 5(c), it was observed that ΔA at 652 nm for GSH was 1.046, the biggest ΔA of other interfering substances was 0.344. Thus, this method also had high selectivity towards GSH, which were likely due to the good antioxidant ability of GSH and anti-interference capability of Pt10-LP NCs [53,54].

Furthermore, the proposed colorimetric method was applied to test GSH concentration in human serum samples. The human serum sample was diluted to half of the original concentration and then added into [Pt10-LP NCs + TMB + H2O2] system. After that, the recoveries of GSH by the standard addition were determined. The recoveries of GSH in human serum sample ranged from 98.41 to 118.06% as shown in Table 4 . Hence, the results suggested that the developed biosensor platform had high accuracy in biological detection field.

In summary, stable platinum nanoclusters with enhanced peroxidase-like activity were prepared using green synthetic method. A new, facile and sensitive method for the colorimetric detection of glucose and GSH using prepared Ptn-LP NCs was developed. Ptn-LP NCs exhibited good stability under harsh condition at least 7 days. Likewise, Pt10-LP NCs had higher affinity towards TMB and H2O2 substrates and performed highly efficient catalysis, and the kinetics of Pt10-LP NCs followed typical Michaelis-Menten equation. The detective concentration of glucose was 2.5–1000 μM with the LOD of 1.89 μM; the detective concentration of GSH was 4–140 μM with the LOD of 0.37 μM. The stability, ultra-small size and LP was good for the detection application of Pt10-LP NCs. Compared with other detection methods based on nanoenzymes, the colorimetric method for detection of glucose and GSH using Ptn-LP NCs had good sensitivity and great potential application in biological detection fields.

Liyuan Fan: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Xianbing Ji: Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Guoqing Lin: Investigation. Kai Liu: Investigation. Shengfu Chen: Writing - review & editing. Guanglong Ma: Writing - review & editing. Weili Xue: Investigation. Xiaoyu Zhang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology. Longgang Wang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from National Nature Science Foundation of China (21674092 and 21975216), National Development Project on Key Basic Research (973 Project, 2015CB655303), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2017203229), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M601284).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106202.
